Cambodia's Context: Cambodia scored a 0.37 score

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No national AI strategy or policy; absent from government agenda
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Draft strategy or formal consultations underway
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: National AI strategy formally published
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): AI strategy actively implemented with defined targets and allocated funding
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Comprehensive, regularly reviewed AI strategy with measurable outcomes and enforcement mechanisms
Cambodia's Context:
The strategy outlines specific outcomes through its six strategic priorities:
-Skilled Workforce: Increased number of AI professionals and researchers through specialized university programs.
-Digital Government Excellence: Enhanced public service delivery through automated, streamlined, and transparent administrative processes.
-Economic Competitiveness: Boosting SME efficiency and modernizing industries (e.g., agricultural resource optimization).
-Khmer Language Inclusion: The successful development of a Khmer Large Language Model (LLM) to ensure AI services are accessible to all Cambodians.
-HPC & Data Sovereignty: Establishing a National AI and Data Science Lab to reduce reliance on external compute resources and store data locally.
-International Standing: Positioning Cambodia as a proactive participant in regional (ASEAN) and global AI governance discussions.

Sub-Parameter: AI Governance and Regulatory Frameworks and Ethical AI Principles
Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No governance framework or regulatory discussion; No ethical AI discussion or principles
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Initial discussions on AI governance; no formal framework; Ethical AI principles under discussion or consultation
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: AI governance guidelines or regulatory approach published; Ethical AI principles formally published
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): AI governance framework operationalised; regulatory body or oversight function designated;Ethical AI principles embedded in procurement, deployment standards, and public-sector mandates
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Comprehensive regulatory regime with active oversight, enforcement, and adaptive review cycles;Ethical AI framework benchmarked internationally; referenced or adopted by peer countries
Cambodia's Context: AI Governance framework a part of recommendations in the UNESCO RAM and also part of the National AI Strategy based on the same
Insight from Kalpa Impact:
1. Opportunity to address and operationalize efforts to democratize access and capacity related to AI through increased focus on programs - specific opportunities could be on the gender, language and urban/rural lines as is the case for the entire ecosystem.
2. Reinforcing operationalized efforts against Deepfakes and misinformation as part of the next phase of their AI National Strategy

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No data protection legislation
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Draft data protection law or consultation underway
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Data protection legislation enacted
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Data protection law enforced with designated authority and complaint mechanisms
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Data protection law enforced with strong redressal mechanism
Cambodia's Context:
the Royal Government of Cambodia issued Sub-Decree No. 252 on the Management, Use, and Protection of Personal Identification Data (only available in Khmer) (Sub-Decree 252) in order to promote broad policy objections, such as - ensuring the protection of peace and order; serving the public interest; and promoting national development by improving the provision of services.
However, Sub-Decree 252 only applies to "personal identification data" owned by the Ministry of Interior (MOI) and does not apply to personal identification data used by other entities.
In September 2023, the MPTC made available to select private organizations and companies a Draft Law on Personal Data Protection for their review and comment. 
A later draft was made available to the Public for consultation in July 2025.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No government AI initiatives or pilots
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Ad hoc exploratory pilots initiated by individual agencies
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Pilot AI projects implemented in select public-sector domains with defined scope
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multiple AI deployments across ministries and public services; government AI programs with funding and targets
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Large-scale AI deployment across government; national AI program with R&D, compute, and talent investment
Cambodia's Context:
NEC and the Cambodian Mine Action Center Successfully Predict Landmine: Traditionally, identifying potential landmine-infested areas required extensive, unorganized data and significant manpower over long periods. By leveraging AI, this process can now be performed quickly with fewer personnel, demonstrating the potential to support swift and efficient landmine clearance. 

National Research Center on AI for Education inaugarated (Nov 2025): The National Research Center on AI for Education was officially inaugurated during the AI for Education National Conference 2025. The Inauguration Ceremony was presided over by His Excellency Dr. Hang Chuon Naron, Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Education, Youth and Sport (MoEYS), and His Excellency Dr. Chea Vandeth, Minister of Post and Telecommunications (MPTC). The establishment of the Center was led by the Rector Council of Cambodia (RCC), in cooperation with the Cambodia Academy of Digital Technology (CADT), and supported by MoEYS and MPTC.

Cambodia and Singapore Partner to Develop Khmer Large Language Model: AI Forum Cambodia and AI Singapore have signed an MoU to collaboratively develop an open-source Khmer Large Language Model, aiming to advance AI capabilities in Southeast Asia by addressing linguistic challenges, fostering skill development and ensuring equitable access to AI for Cambodians
Insight from Kalpa Impact: 1. KhmerLLM program (in partnership with Singapore) development opportunity for UNDP.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No national DT strategy or roadmap
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Draft DT strategy or consultations underway
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: National DT strategy formally published with implementation roadmap
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): DT strategy actively implemented with targets, timelines, and dedicated budget
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: DT strategy mature, regularly updated, and used as a model for regional peers
Cambodia's Context:
The strategy is implemented in three distinct phases, each with specific envisioned goals:
-Phase 1 (2021–2025) – Building Foundations: * Establishment of the "National Council for Digital Economy and Society."
-Successful deployment of digital identity (E-ID) and payment gateways (e.g., Bakong).
-Enactment of essential digital laws and updated regulations.

-Phase 2 (2026–2030) – Digital Adoption: * Mass adoption of digital services by citizens and businesses.
-Modernization of all key state systems and public services.
-Strengthened regional (ASEAN) and global digital cooperation.

-Phase 3 (2031–2035) – Digital Transformation: * Transformation into a fully participatory digital society where technology drives economic productivity and innovation.
-A sizable digital economy contributing significantly to the national GDP.
-A highly skilled workforce specializing in advanced fields like AI, Data Analytics, and IoT.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No national digital ID system
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Digital ID system under design or piloting
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Operational national digital ID with basic authentication; enrollment underway
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Digital ID with biometric coverage, multi-factor authentication, and interoperability with at least one other DPI
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Fully interoperable digital ID with high population coverage, privacy safeguards, and federated authentication
Cambodia's Context: Cambodia has a population of 18 Million - CamDigiKey app has only been downloaded 50K+ times. DPI Map marks the CamDigiKey as DPI-like.
Insight from Kalpa Impact: Adoption for the CamDigiKey stand to be improved through usecases once there is a clear understanding of the cause for these low adoption rates.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No national digital payments infrastructure
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Digital payments system in design or early pilot
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Operational digital payments system with basic interoperability
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Interoperable digital payments across financial institutions; growing financial inclusion; significant transaction volumes
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Universal, fully interoperable digital payments; high financial inclusion; integrated with regional payment systems
Cambodia's Context: Digital Payments Bakong - DPI Like with ~100 Million transactions per month, 60% adoption rate.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No data exchange infrastructure or framework

Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Cross-agency data exchange operational with consent mechanisms and defined standards

Cambodia's Context: Data Exchange Cam-DX - DPI Like. Used across 22 Ministries.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No DPI assets operational; no use cases in public or private sector
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Use case of a DPI asset is being planned
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Use cases of DPI assets across 2–3 sectors in pilot or expansion stage
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multiple use cases across 4+ sectors, cross-DPI integration in public service delivery
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Use case deployment across all government schemes and private sector innovation
Cambodia's Context:
CamDigiKey used for e-KYC authentication across Financial sector and e-Government services. 

Currently majorly adopted use-cases are related to Business Registration.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): Electricity access <70%;unreliable grid
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Electricity access <70%; national electrification roadmap or grid investment actively underway
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Electricity access >80%
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Electricity access >95%; stable grid nationally
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Near-universal electricity supply
Cambodia's Context: Electricity access - 92.3%
Insight from Kalpa Impact: Access to cloud and data centres to be assessed

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): Internet penetration <70%; significant urban-rural gap
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Internet penetration <70%; active connectivity expansion program
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Internet penetration >80%
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Internet penetration >95%
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Near-universal internet
Cambodia's Context: ~60% internet penetration. While mobile broadband coverage exists, the actual internet speeds rank low globally (73rd for mobile broadband and 107th for fixed broadband), impeding data-heavy AI operations

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No cloud or data centre presence; no domestic hosting capacity
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Nascent or imported cloud services only; no domestic compute infrastructure
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Basic cloud or data hosting via domestic or regional providers
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Compute-capable national data centres operational; domestic and international cloud available
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Availability of compute- govt effort, data centres
Cambodia's Context: 4 modern data centres established - cloud services sparcely available. A massive hurdle is the lack of modern data centers and domestic High-Performance Computing (HPC) capabilities. A survey found that only 11% of surveyed institutions have on-premises GPU processors

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): Large access gaps across geographic, gender, and socioeconomic dimensions; no disaggregated data collected
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Inclusion gaps identified and measured; pilot programmes or targeted interventions initiated
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Inclusion programmes operational;gaps narrowing with measurable progress
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Gaps significantly narrowed- rural-urban gap <5 percentage points, gender gap <5 percentage points; affrodable infrastructure available nationally
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Near equitable access across all dimensions
Cambodia's Context:
Stark gap in Urban-Rural Internet connectivity, with ~60% of Cambodia's population using Internet, but usage being concentrated amongst young urban people. 

While statistics around the digital restrictions of Rural Women are available through the UNICEF Girl's Digital Literacy Program's report.
Insight from Kalpa Impact: Inclusion based programs for digital literacy, few programs already exist within SEA which can be re-utilized for Cambodia

Sub-Parameter:
SK  - RECENT INDICATOR /Strategic and Long-term 
Subjective call - KIIs
Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): High instability or conflict; no digital transformation agenda
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Fragile stability; digital agenda exists but is ad hoc and leadership-dependent
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Stable government; digital transformation is a formal priority and survives a change of administration
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Sustained stability; digital transformation embedded in national agenda across election cycles
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Full stability, digital transformation is a cross-party strategic priority
Cambodia's Context: Conflict between Cambodia and Thailand could escalate in the coming months
Insight from Kalpa Impact: Not an insight - Cambodia's One-party system on a national level has prioritized DT, especially post-2020. Though, if the one-party system sees any amount of civil unrest - could adversely affect the priorities, they might shift towards National Security first.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No designated lead agency for AI/DPI; no formal initiative; no cross-government coordination
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Single ministry or agency leading digital transformation; no cross-government coordination mechanism
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Inter-ministerial coordination mechanism established; lead agency with clear mandate
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Institutionalised inter-ministerial platforms with recurring mandates; regulatory sandboxes operational; CDO leading coordination
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Whole-of-government architecture with clear roles across national and sector agencies; continuous, transparent coordination
Cambodia's Context: No single agency responsible for the overall Digital Transformation, for eg. Ministry of Post and Telecommunications responsible for AI Strategy while DPI managed by different ministries.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No private sector engagement in digital agenda
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Bilateral engagement with select tech or telecom operators; largely ad hoc and commercial
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Structured consultations with private sector formally documented in strategy development; PPP frameworks emerging
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Formal public-private platforms operational; DPG and open-source ecosystem engaged; fintech and cloud providers integrated in delivery
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Deep ecosystem integration; private sector co-invests in national digital infrastructure; DPGs and open standards embedded in public procurement
Cambodia's Context: Techo startup center usually works for the implementation of DPIs as a private sector actor. Techo startup center works as an incubation/accelerator for startups in Cambodia supported by the government, but privately owned.
Insight from Kalpa Impact: Structured consultation with private sector actors in the implementation space required for furthering the open-source developement of DPGs, this private actor identification (intl or local) can be facilitated by UNDP

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No MDB or bilateral engagement; no pipeline financing identified
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: MDB/donor activity exploratory; primarily providing advisory or strategy support
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Recurrent MDB and bilateral investment in AI/DPI projects; approved and pipeline financing visible
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multi-year strategic partnerships with ADB, World Bank, bilaterals operational
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Development partners fully aligned to national roadmap
Cambodia's Context: ~$3.1B in concessional loans/SDRs provided to ADB; UNESCO provides technical expertise via "AI Ethics Experts without Borders."

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No domestic academic or research engagement in AI/DPI
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Isolated university or think tank activity; no formal linkage to government agenda
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: At least one institution formally engaged in AI/DPI research; outputs informing policy discussions
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multiple universities and think tanks contributing to AI/DPI policy; formal research-government linkage mechanisms
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: National research ecosystem; AI/DPI research institutions internationally recognised; research actively shaping national and regional policy
Cambodia's Context: CADT actively involved in the National AI Strategy, also a part of the country's capacity building efforts
Insight from Kalpa Impact: CADT may be a potential collaborator for UNDP - CADT can be actively involved in inclusive capacity building programs to make sure there is equitable representation in research centres for education and other sectors in the future

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No civil society engagement; digital rights not on agenda
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Civil society informed but not consulted; awareness-level engagement only
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Civil society formally consulted on governance and ethics dimensions; inputs recorded and acknowledged
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Independent digital rights organisations active; civil society participates in governance and ethics discussions with formal channels
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Civil society embedded in co-design and oversight; transparent public accountability mechanisms for AI and DPI governance
Cambodia's Context:
1. Cambodian Development Innovations Project: Since the Development Innovations project began in 2013, internet penetration in Cambodia has skyrocketed from just 6.8 percent to 56.7 percent in 2024. Civil society, MSMEs, and the Royal Government of Cambodia are leveraging tools like Trey Visay to expand services and use social media and videos to tap into new markets and increase incomes
2. Consultations from Civil Societies actively seeked during the formulation of the National AI Strategy by the Ministry of Post and Telecommunications.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No dedicated AI/DPI budget line; digital transformation funded opportunistically or not at all
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Ministry nominally responsible; limited, non-recurrent annual allocation; insufficient for implementation
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Dedicated recurring domestic budget for AI/DPI development and upkeep; responsible ministry funded with multi-year commitment
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Sustained multi-year public investment programme; budget execution rates tracked and reported; domestic co-financing of externally-supported initiatives
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Long-term sovereign funding mechanisms; significant R&D allocation; domestic digital transformation budget substantial and independently sustained
Cambodia's Context:
1. Ministry of Post and Telecommunications (MPTC), the Ministry of Industry, Science, Technology & Innovation (MISTI), and the Digital Economy and Business Committee are actively responsible for executing major, well-defined roadmaps like the Cambodia Digital Economy and Society Policy Framework 2021–2035 and the Digital Government Policy 2022–2035. 

2. Actual domestic public funding earmarked specifically for Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) and AI development remains limited, with a heavy reliance on Development-based external assistance for digital transformation. While Cambodia has multi-year policy strategies, it does not yet have a guaranteed, sustained multi-year domestic fiscal budget line strictly for the technical upkeep and development of AI/DPI. Funding is still largely approved on an annual, constrained basis or tied to specific foreign-funded project cycles.
